topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the glossy printout spread across his worn wooden desk. The typeface, the carefully chosen anecdotes, the very cadence of his triumphs and missteps—it all belonged to him, yet it felt like a costume someone had forced onto him. His hands began to tremble, the paper crinkling loudly under the pressure of his grip. He reread the passage describing his 'breakthrough' in astrophysics, the description so vivid and personal it made his skin prickle with a strange, hot flush creeping up his neck. He slowly pushed the document away, the sound a sharp, definitive snap in the quiet room. He stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of his study, his breathing shallow and quick. Every shadow seemed to stretch and distort, and he kept catching his reflection in the dark windowpane, his face a mask of utter bewilderment. "This isn't me," he whispered to the empty air, the sound thin and brittle.

[story 2]

Clara felt a sudden, icy dread coil tight in her stomach as she scanned the official biographical summary online. The narrative flowed so smoothly, detailing her early struggles and eventual accolades, but the voice in the text sounded utterly alien. Her jaw tightened, and she pressed the heels of her palms against her eyes, trying to physically push the information out of her sight. A low, frustrated groan escaped her lips, a sound of profound deflation. She snatched her phone, her fingers fumbling over the screen as she tried to find the source, her movements jerky and uncontrolled. When she finally found the source—a forgotten university archive—she let out a shaky exhale, leaning back in her chair until the worn leather creaked under her weight. Her gaze dropped to her hands, noticing the slight tremor in her fingers, a physical manifestation of the sheer awkwardness of the situation.

[story 3]

Marcus sat rigidly on the edge of the sofa, the printed biography resting on his lap like a lead weight. He kept glancing up, his eyes darting around the room as if expecting the author to materialize and demand an explanation. A deep, involuntary shudder ran through him, starting in his spine and radiating outward. He cleared his throat repeatedly, the sound rough and unused. "No," he managed to articulate, the word barely a puff of air. He tried to smooth the paper flat with his thumb, a futile attempt to restore some semblance of order to the chaos unfolding in his mind. The heat rising in his cheeks felt overwhelming, a visible stain against his skin. He stood up, intending to walk away, but his legs felt strangely weighted, anchoring him to the spot, unwilling to face the world until he understood who had been speaking for him.
